md: ### Spinning a shape around a point
A **rotation** turns a shape around a fixed point (the **center of rotation**) by a specific angle. Every point of the shape sweeps out an arc of a circle centered at that point.
If the angle is positive, the rotation is **counterclockwise** (mathematical convention). Negative angles rotate clockwise. The center of rotation stays put — it's the only point that doesn't move.
The orange dot is the center of rotation (here, the origin). Slide the angle and watch the shape spin around it. At 360° (or -360°) it returns to the start.

blocks.title: The special angles to memorize
blocks.body:
**Rotation by 90° counterclockwise** about origin: $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$.
**Rotation by 180°** about origin: $(x, y) \to (-x, -y)$.
**Rotation by 270° counterclockwise** (= 90° clockwise) about origin: $(x, y) \to (y, -x)$.
**Rotation by 360°** about origin: identity (back to start).

md: Rotation by angle $\theta$ about the origin uses trigonometry:

blocks.title: Rotating about a center other than the origin
blocks.body:
To rotate around an arbitrary center $(h, k)$:
1. **Translate** so $(h, k)$ moves to the origin: subtract $(h, k)$.
2. **Rotate** by $\theta$ about the origin.
3. **Translate back**: add $(h, k)$.

It's like sliding a piece of paper so the rotation point is at the origin, spinning, and sliding back. Three simple steps.

md: **Preserved:** distances, angles, side lengths, area, orientation.
Rotations are **rigid motions** like translations — congruent shapes. Unlike reflections, rotations preserve orientation (a clockwise polygon stays clockwise after rotation).
Rotate $A(3, 1)$ by 90° counterclockwise about the origin.
**Check by drawing:** $A$ is 3 right, 1 up. After 90° counterclockwise, that becomes 1 left, 3 up — yes, $(-1, 3)$. ✓

blocks.title: Rotational symmetry
blocks.body:
A shape has **rotational symmetry of order $n$** if rotating it by $360°/n$ about its center sends the shape to itself.

**Equilateral triangle:** order 3 (120°, 240°, 360°).
**Square:** order 4 (90°, 180°, 270°, 360°).
**Regular hexagon:** order 6 (60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°, 360°).
**Circle:** infinite (any angle works).

A shape with order 1 has no rotational symmetry (only the trivial 360° return).
